metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Evidence claim: replace 'finalize_verdict does NOT read any attribute off its ctx argument' with the accurate statement that the verdict STRING computation uses only coverage flags (not ctx attributes), while acknowledging that finalize_verdict does read ctx.repo_root, ctx.ticket_id, and ctx.ticket_type for output fields — and that the three minimal-PlanContext call sites already accept ctx.repo_root=None (guide_url falls back to base URL).
evidence: Plan quotes: 'it does NOT read any attribute off its ctx: PlanContext argument today — confirming coverage (not the ctx object) is the correct, established plumbing point'
orchestrator.py:555: `_base = _config.plan_review_docs_url(ctx.repo_root)` — reads ctx.repo_root
orchestrator.py:569: `'ticket_id': ctx.ticket_id` — reads ctx.ticket_id
orchestrator.py:570: `'ticket_type': ctx.ticket_type` — reads ctx.ticket_type

The minimal-PlanContext sites (workflow_ops.py:540-544, gate_dispatch.py:330-334, gate_dispatch.py:390-395) all omit repo_root, so ctx.repo_root=None at those three sites — this is pre-existing behavior but is invisible given the false claim.
findings.finding: The plan's Evidence section asserts 'finalize_verdict does NOT read any attribute off its `ctx: PlanContext` argument today — confirming `coverage` (not the `ctx` object) is the correct, established plumbing point for a new flag.' This is factually wrong: `finalize_verdict` (orchestrator.py:555, 569, 570) reads `ctx.repo_root`, `ctx.ticket_id`, and `ctx.ticket_type` in its body. The conclusion (use coverage dict for hierarchy_incomplete) is still sound for other reasons (the verdict STRING logic uses only coverage flags), but the stated evidence is a verifiably false codebase claim.
findings.location: Evidence section: 'finalize_verdict ... does NOT read any attribute off its ctx: PlanContext argument today'; orchestrator.py:555, 569, 570
scenarios: An implementer reading the Evidence section to understand why coverage-dict threading is preferred over a ctx attribute may construct a false mental model, potentially missing that ctx.repo_root is None at the three minimal-PlanContext sites (coach + two recovery paths) — which already causes guide_url to fall back to the base URL for those verdicts, a side-effect the plan silently accepts.

If a future change adds logic to finalize_verdict that reads ctx.hierarchy_incomplete directly (thinking the plan sanctioned ctx-attribute reads), it would fail at the three minimal-pctx call sites where hierarchy_incomplete is not set on the reconstructed PlanContext.
findings.suggested_fix: Update Evidence to: 'finalize_verdict reads ctx.repo_root (line 555, for guide_url), ctx.ticket_id, and ctx.ticket_type (lines 569-570, for the verdict dict), but the VERDICT STRING (BLOCK/INDETERMINATE/PASS) is computed solely from coverage flags — confirming coverage is the correct plumbing point for hierarchy_incomplete. The three minimal-PlanContext sites (plan_review_coach, _recover_plan_review_coach_failure, _recover_plan_review_verify_failure) already pass repo_root=None, causing guide_url to fall back to the base URL — pre-existing behavior unchanged by this ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to each AC: (a) for the retry AC, name the test module and a `pytest -k` selector; (b) for the YAML AC, add a grep anchor such as `grep -c 'hierarchy_incomplete' src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml` asserting count ≥ 8 (2 fields × 4 `with:` blocks); (c) for the test-coverage AC, name the test file path so `pytest <path>` can be formed without inference.
evidence: AC items 1-10 each describe an observable state ('PlanContext gains...', '...retries once...', '...each gain an elif...') but contain no executable command producing evidence.
AC item for YAML: 'All four uses: plan_review_coach steps in src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml … have their with: block extended…' — no grep or lint_refs proving command.

AC item for tests: 'Test coverage includes: (a) total-enumeration-failure -> hierarchy_incomplete_detail == ["enumeration"] exactly; (b) partial per-child failure…' — no pytest path or test module named.
Plan mentions lint_refs.py:445-450 as a ref-checker in Evidence but never says 'run lint_refs on plan-review.yaml' as a proving step in any AC.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command (pytest invocation, grep anchor, or rebar lint command) is provided for any acceptance criterion. All ACs describe code/schema/YAML states in prose but supply no executable command that would produce pass/fail evidence in-session. In particular: (a) the retry behavior AC ('2 attempts total, 50ms fixed delay') has no test file path or pytest command; (b) the YAML edit AC ('all four uses: plan_review_coach steps … extended with hierarchy_incomplete/hierarchy_incomplete_detail') has no grep or lint_refs invocation to confirm all four sites were updated — a partially-applied edit (3 of 4 steps) would silently defeat coach-path threading for one branch arm and would be undetectable without a named check; (c) the test-coverage AC names assertions but provides no file path, so `pytest <path>` cannot be formed without inference.
scenarios: An executor updates 3 of 4 YAML `with:` blocks (missing the `else` arm of one branch); all unit tests pass; no grep command exists in the ACs to catch the gap; the plan_review_coach step on that branch arm silently defaults hierarchy_incomplete to False.
An executor writes the retry logic but uses 1 attempt (no retry) due to a misread; no pytest command targets this specific behavior; CI passes on unrelated tests; the AC is marked done incorrectly.
A code reviewer cannot confirm done-ness independently because the ACs supply no reproducible check.
findings.suggested_fix: For the YAML AC append: 'Proving command: `grep -c "hierarchy_incomplete" src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml` must return ≥ 8 (two new keys × four with: blocks).' For the retry AC append: 'Proving command: `pytest tests/unit/plan_review/test_context_assembly.py -k "incomplete"` must pass with assertions on attempt count and detail values.' For the test-coverage AC, specify the target file, e.g. `tests/unit/plan_review/test_hierarchy_incomplete.py`, and add `pytest tests/unit/plan_review/test_hierarchy_incomplete.py` as the proving command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` to the inline `coverage` dict at `workflow_ops.py:213` (the P8-too-big `finalize_verdict` call inside `plan_review_precheck`), separately from the `base` dict update — the plan's current remediation conflates these two distinct dicts.
evidence: Remediation item 2, bullet 1: 'plan_review_precheck (workflow_ops.py:135), call site line 209: real pctx in scope -> coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"] = pctx.hierarchy_incomplete ... Also add both fields to the base dict (line 141-147)'

Code at workflow_ops.py:209-216: `verdict = orchestrator.finalize_verdict(pctx, parts, coaching=[], coverage={"det": det_cov, "llm_ran": False}, ...)` — the coverage dict here is an inline literal, entirely separate from the `base` dict at lines 141-147

The `base` dict (lines 141-147) flows into `{**base, "run_llm": False, "verdict": verdict}` — this is the STEP OUTPUT dict the plan says to update for downstream threading, correctly; but separately the `finalize_verdict` coverage at line 213 embeds `hierarchy_incomplete` inside the BLOCK verdict itself, and is not the same object as `base`
findings.finding: The plan's Remediation item 2, bullet 1 conflates two distinct dicts in `plan_review_precheck`: the `base` dict (line 141-147, which populates the step's OUTPUT and flows to downstream `ctx.inputs`) and the `coverage` dict passed inline to `finalize_verdict` at line 213 (`coverage={"det": det_cov, "llm_ran": False}`). The plan says to add the new fields to 'its `base`/`coverage` dicts, used at its own `finalize_verdict` call (line 209)', implying these are the same entity. They are not — the P8-too-big path builds its own separate literal `coverage` dict at line 213, distinct from `base`. Without explicitly targeting this inline dict, the embedded BLOCK verdict produced by the P8-too-big `finalize_verdict` call will never carry `hierarchy_incomplete`, even though the step output dict (from `base`) will.
findings.location: Remediation item 2, bullet 1 / workflow_ops.py:202-217 (p8_too_big branch, finalize_verdict call at line 209 with coverage at line 213)
scenarios: A container ticket whose child enumeration fails (hierarchy_incomplete=True) AND whose plan is P8-too-big triggers the p8_too_big branch. The `base` dict correctly carries `hierarchy_incomplete=True` for downstream steps, but the embedded BLOCK verdict's own coverage (line 213) does not — so the verdict itself doesn't signal `hierarchy_incomplete` and the AC check 'verdict is INDETERMINATE at at least one finalize_verdict site' could fail for this path.

The P8-too-big path is a short-circuit (ELSE arm, no LLM runs), so this verdict is the terminal output. If `hierarchy_incomplete` is missing from its `finalize_verdict` coverage, downstream consumers reading `verdict.coverage.hierarchy_incomplete` will see it absent even when enumeration failed.
findings.suggested_fix: In Remediation item 2, bullet 1, split the instruction: (a) add both fields to the `base` dict (lines 141-147) for downstream step threading, and (b) separately add `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"] = pctx.hierarchy_incomplete` before the `finalize_verdict` call at line 209 — where `coverage` refers to the inline literal dict `{"det": det_cov, "llm_ran": False}` that must be built as a variable first, then passed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `elif coverage.get('hierarchy_incomplete')` branch is inserted BEFORE `elif indeterminate and not surfaced` (i.e., as the third branch after `verify_failed`), so it fires regardless of whether surfaced advisories are present.
evidence: Plan text: 'add `elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"` alongside the existing `llm_unavailable`/`verify_failed` branches (same `elif` chain, checked before falling through to PASS)'

Existing code at orchestrator.py:538: `elif indeterminate and not surfaced: verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` — this branch is also 'before falling through to PASS' but would NOT cover `hierarchy_incomplete=True` with surfaced advisories present
Concrete miss: pctx.hierarchy_incomplete=True + parts['surfaced'] non-empty → `indeterminate and not surfaced` is False → if new branch is after it, falls to `else: PASS`
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding `elif coverage.get('hierarchy_incomplete'): verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` to `finalize_verdict`'s elif chain but specifies only 'checked before falling through to PASS', which is ambiguous: the existing chain ends with `elif indeterminate and not surfaced: verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` then `else: verdict = 'PASS'`. If the new branch is placed AFTER `elif indeterminate and not surfaced`, a ticket with `hierarchy_incomplete=True` AND non-empty `surfaced` advisories (so `indeterminate and not surfaced` is falsy) silently reaches `else: PASS` instead of INDETERMINATE — exactly the clean-pass scenario the ticket aims to prevent.
scenarios: A container ticket whose child enumeration fails but the LLM still surfaces advisory findings (surfaced non-empty) — if `hierarchy_incomplete` branch is placed after `elif indeterminate and not surfaced`, verdict = PASS despite incomplete hierarchy.
findings.suggested_fix: Remediation item 3 should read: 'In `finalize_verdict` (orchestrator.py:503-541), insert `elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"` as the THIRD `elif` — immediately after the `verify_failed` branch and BEFORE the `elif indeterminate and not surfaced` branch.'

evidence: 7 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether `hierarchy_incomplete=True` should force INDETERMINATE for exempt-type and bug-tier tickets, or whether these ticket types should be carved out (e.g., by not setting `hierarchy_incomplete` in `pctx` when `ticket_type in ('session_log','code_review','identity','bug')`, or by not threading it through those early-return paths).
evidence: Plan text (Remediation item 2): 'plan_review_precheck (workflow_ops.py:135) reads pctx.hierarchy_incomplete... and threads both into its base/coverage dicts, used at its own finalize_verdict call (line 209)'
`_exempt_verdict` at orchestrator.py:721-735 hardcodes `'verdict': 'PASS'` and `'coverage': {'exempt': reason}` — no `hierarchy_incomplete` read path exists or is specified
Bug-tier return at workflow_ops.py:171-178 sets `verdict: None` (run_llm=True) — `finalize_verdict` is never called on this path, so the new `elif` branch there is unreachable

ABSENCE: no plan text addresses what `hierarchy_incomplete=True` means for exempt-type tickets (where P5/P8 container checks are irrelevant by ticket-type definition) or for bugs (which are never blocked by design)
findings.finding: The plan threads `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` through `plan_review_precheck`'s `finalize_verdict` call (the P8-too-big path at line 209) and into the `base` dict — but says nothing about the two other early-return paths in the same function: the exempt-type return (lines 150-157, which calls `_exempt_verdict` and hardcodes `verdict: 'PASS'` without reading `hierarchy_incomplete`) and the bug-tier return (lines 166-178, which returns `verdict: None` and `run_llm: True` but never calls `finalize_verdict`). An executor adding `hierarchy_incomplete` to `base` propagates the field into these returns' step-output dict, but `_exempt_verdict`'s hardcoded PASS is never overridden. No explicit decision is given for whether hierarchy failure on exempt-type or bug-tier tickets should yield INDETERMINATE or remain PASS/None.
findings.location: Remediation item 2, `workflow_ops.py:150-178` (`_exempt_verdict` call and bug-tier early return)
scenarios: An exempt-type ticket (session_log) whose `list_tickets` call fails: `hierarchy_incomplete=True` is set on `pctx`, propagated into the step-output `base` dict, but `_exempt_verdict` still returns `verdict: PASS` — recovery paths reading `precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete')` would see `True` and call `finalize_verdict` with it, potentially yielding INDETERMINATE for an exempt ticket.

A bug-type ticket with hierarchy failure: `hierarchy_incomplete=True` flows into `base` and downstream, but `finalize_verdict` is never called in `plan_review_precheck` for a bug, so the new INDETERMINATE branch never fires at precheck time — the flag travels to coach recovery paths where it would unexpectedly trigger INDETERMINATE for a ticket type that is designed to always yield PASS.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note or TODO in the remediation spec (or a fixture/RED test) that probes whether the local ticket-store actually produces transient failures that resolve within 50 ms, OR document that the 50 ms delay is deliberately conservative and acceptable even if retries always fail (i.e., the flag-setting path is the intended outcome, not retry success).
evidence: "wrap the `list_tickets` call (line 174) in a bounded retry: **2 attempts total** (1 retry), catching the same broad `Exception` it catches today, with a **fixed 50ms delay** between attempts"

Evidence section: 'No reusable predicate/backoff precedent applies to this call; a bespoke tiny retry is justified' — justified by absence of precedent, not by measurement of actual transient-failure rate or recovery time for local ticket-store reads.
The ticket-store read (`list_by_query`, `show_ticket`) is a local call, not HTTP; whether transient failures (a) occur at all in practice, and (b) resolve within 50 ms, is unvalidated.
findings.finding: The 50 ms fixed retry delay and 2-attempt retry count for `list_tickets`/`show_ticket` in `_assemble_context_uncached` are asserted without empirical derivation or a plan to validate them against actual failure modes of the local ticket store.
scenarios: If the ticket-store failure mode is permanent (e.g., misconfigured tracker, missing index), the retry adds 50 ms of latency with no benefit and the flag is set anyway — harmless but untested.
If transient failures require more than 50 ms to resolve (e.g., a lock contention scenario), the retry succeeds 0% of the time, making the retry logic dead code in practice.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a comment in Remediation item 1 clarifying that the retry is defensive and the expected steady-state is that it rarely succeeds (making the delay value irrelevant), so 0 ms / immediate retry is equally valid; or (b) add a fixture test that injects a one-shot failure (first call raises, second succeeds) and asserts that the retry actually recovers, validating the retry-success path before freeze.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test case for the transient-failure-then-success path: first `list_tickets` call raises, second call succeeds → `hierarchy_incomplete` is `False`, `children` is populated, and the final verdict is not INDETERMINATE (verifies the retry mechanism's success branch, not just exhaustion).
evidence: AC test coverage specifies: '(a) total-enumeration-failure -> hierarchy_incomplete_detail == ["enumeration"] exactly; (b) partial per-child failure -> hierarchy_incomplete_detail contains the specific failed child id; (c) both cases -> verdict is INDETERMINATE'
No AC or test-case assertion requires: first attempt raises, second attempt succeeds → hierarchy_incomplete remains False and children is populated.
Remediation item 1 specifies '2 attempts total, 50ms fixed delay' but the retry-succeeds behavioral invariant is absent from the test plan.
findings.finding: The test plan covers only the retry-exhaustion case (all attempts fail → flag set). It does not require a test for the transient-failure-then-success case: if the first `list_tickets` call raises but the second succeeds, `hierarchy_incomplete` must NOT be set and children must be populated normally. Without this test, an incorrect implementation (e.g., setting the flag on any first exception regardless of retry outcome) would pass all specified tests while silently over-flagging valid hierarchy loads as INDETERMINATE.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last bullet (test coverage), Remediation item 1
scenarios: A ticket store that is transiently unavailable (e.g. momentary timeout) succeeds on retry; hierarchy_incomplete is incorrectly set to True; the verdict is INDETERMINATE for a plan that actually had its full context loaded.
findings.suggested_fix: In the test suite covering `_assemble_context_uncached`, add a case using a mock that raises on the first call and returns valid data on the second; assert that the returned `PlanContext` has `hierarchy_incomplete == False` and `children` is non-empty. Apply the same transient-success test to the per-child `show_ticket` retry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete back-out procedure for the `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` schema change: specify the post-rollback schema state explicitly (e.g., 'the two fields are removed from `properties`; any records written between deploy and rollback that contain these fields will fail `additionalProperties: false` validation') and provide a remediation step (e.g., 'before reverting, archive or strip affected records using `rebar admin purge-step-outputs --step precheck --since <deploy-sha>`', or 'relax `additionalProperties` to `true` in the rollback target to tolerate extra fields in existing records').
evidence: Plan quote: 'if this schema change is ever reverted, run records already written by the new code (containing hierarchy_incomplete/hierarchy_incomplete_detail) would fail the reverted schema's closed additionalProperties: false validation when read back by gate_dispatch.py's recovery paths'
Plan quote: 'This is an accepted tradeoff, not a gap to remediate here'

No back-out procedure is specified (e.g., 'before reverting the schema, delete or archive records written between deploy and rollback', or 'add a migration step that strips the two fields from existing records')
T11 criterion: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0 — a rollback can exit 0 while leaving the schema in an incorrect intermediate state'
findings.finding: The plan adds `hierarchy_incomplete` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` to `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` (which has `additionalProperties: false`) and acknowledges that reverting the schema would break reads of any records written by the new code — but it provides no concrete back-out path, only declaring the risk an 'accepted tradeoff.' T11 requires the plan to assert the schema STATE after rollback AND supply a back-out path, not merely acknowledge the breakage.
scenarios: Operator deploys the change, a batch of plan-review runs executes and writes records with `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` fields, then a rollback is performed reverting the schema. Subsequently, `gate_dispatch.py` recovery paths (which call `precheck = succeeded.get(STEP_PRECHECK)`) attempt to validate or read those records against the reverted schema with `additionalProperties: false` and fail — the recovery paths are now broken for any run that was in-flight or recorded during the deploy window.

An automated rollback triggered by an unrelated incident leaves the system with written records it can no longer read, causing all recovery-path verdicts for those runs to error rather than degrade gracefully.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` change — either an expand-contract sequence (e.g., temporarily widen `additionalProperties` during transition, then re-close after all consumers are updated) or a concrete procedure for handling records written by the new code if the schema change must be reverted.
evidence: Plan states: 'if this schema change is ever reverted, run records already written by the new code (containing hierarchy_incomplete/hierarchy_incomplete_detail) would fail the reverted schema's closed additionalProperties: false validation when read back by gate_dispatch.py's recovery paths'
Plan states: 'This is an accepted tradeoff, not a gap to remediate here'
Plan states: 'schema field additions in this codebase are forward-only (no prior art for a schema-rollback data migration)'
No expand-contract sequence (e.g., temporarily set additionalProperties: true, deploy, then re-close) or explicit rollback procedure is described anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The schema change (`plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` adding two new properties under `additionalProperties: false`) is explicitly acknowledged as irreversible — rollback would cause validation failures for records written by the new code — but the plan provides only an acceptance statement ('an accepted tradeoff, not a gap to remediate here') with no rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing. Per T4, acknowledgement alone is insufficient; a reversibility mechanism is required.
scenarios: A hotfix rollback of the schema change is needed after deployment; existing stored run records with the new fields fail schema validation in the recovery paths, causing gate_dispatch to error on previously-recorded step outputs
A partial deployment leaves some workers on old schema and some on new, with the new fields present in some records but not others
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Rollback section: (1) expand-contract: before deploying code that writes the new fields, set `additionalProperties: true` in the schema so old and new records both validate; after all consumers are updated to read the new fields, re-close to `additionalProperties: false` with the new properties declared. This makes the change safely reversible at any step. Alternatively, if the codebase genuinely has no rollback path, document the explicit decision: 'a rollback of this schema change requires either (a) deleting all run records written after deployment or (b) keeping additionalProperties: true permanently,' so the on-call engineer has a defined procedure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `_HIERARCHY_FETCH_RETRIES` and `_HIERARCHY_FETCH_DELAY_S` (or equivalent named constants) at module level in `orchestrator.py` and reference them in both the `list_tickets` and `show_ticket` retry implementations, so the values are tunable in one place.
evidence: "**2 attempts total** (1 retry)" and "**fixed 50ms delay**" specified as literal values with no reference to a named constant or config entry.
AC does not include a requirement to define these as named constants (e.g., `_HIERARCHY_FETCH_RETRIES = 2`, `_HIERARCHY_FETCH_DELAY_S = 0.05`).
findings.finding: The retry count (2 attempts) and fixed delay (50 ms) are hardcoded inline in the remediation spec with no mention of named constants, making them invisible to future tuners and inconsistent with how other thresholds in the codebase are managed.
scenarios: A future engineer adjusting retry behavior for a slow ticket backend must grep for the magic number 50 across the file rather than updating a single named constant.
If a second fetch site is added (e.g., fetching grandchildren), the developer may use a different literal, creating inconsistency.
findings.suggested_fix: Add at the top of the relevant section of `orchestrator.py`: `_HIERARCHY_FETCH_RETRIES: int = 2` and `_HIERARCHY_FETCH_DELAY_S: float = 0.050`, then pass these into the retry loop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `logger.warning(...)` call (or equivalent structured log) at each new exception-catch site in `_assemble_context_uncached` — one for total enumeration failure (logging the exception and `canonical`), one for each per-child fetch failure (logging the exception and child id) — so operators can observe the failure mode in logs without inspecting the verdict payload.
evidence: Remediation item 1 describes catching exceptions and setting `pctx.hierarchy_incomplete = True` / appending to `hierarchy_incomplete_detail`, but contains no mention of `logger.warning(...)` or equivalent instrumentation at the catch site.

The INDETERMINATE verdict is observable in the output, but the root cause (which exception, from which child id, at what time) is not recorded anywhere that operators can see in real-time (e.g., logs/traces).
findings.finding: The plan adds two new catch-and-continue paths in `_assemble_context_uncached` (for `list_tickets` and `show_ticket` failures) but specifies no log/warning emission at those catch sites, leaving operators with no way to observe *why* a plan-review verdict became INDETERMINATE without inspecting the verdict detail payload.
scenarios: An operator sees a flood of INDETERMINATE verdicts and cannot determine whether it is a ticket-store outage, a permission error, or a data issue — they must read the verdict detail payload rather than tailing logs.
A transient failure that self-heals is never noticed because no warning was emitted during the window it was occurring.
findings.suggested_fix: At the `except Exception as exc:` block for `list_tickets`, add `logger.warning("hierarchy enumeration failed for %s, marking incomplete: %s", canonical, exc)`. At the per-child catch, add `logger.warning("failed to fetch child %s for %s, using summary row: %s", child_id, canonical, exc)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollout guard (feature flag or env-var) that controls whether hierarchy-fetch failures produce INDETERMINATE vs. the previous silent-PASS behavior, and document a cheap rollback procedure (flag-flip or image-revert steps) so operators can restore the prior behavior without data cleanup.
evidence: Remediation item 5: 'if this schema change is ever reverted, run records already written by the new code... would fail the reverted schema's closed additionalProperties: false validation when read back by gate_dispatch.py's recovery paths. This is an accepted tradeoff, not a gap to remediate here'
No feature flag, canary, or percentage-rollout mechanism is mentioned anywhere in the plan
No rollback procedure (e.g., deploy old image, run a migration, truncate records) is specified
The behavioral delta — any ticket whose child enumeration or fetch fails now blocks at INDETERMINATE instead of silently passing — affects all workflow runs immediately on deploy
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant behavioral change (silent PASS → INDETERMINATE for all hierarchy-fetch failures) with no staged rollout mechanism (flag, canary, or percentage rollout) and no explicit cheap rollback path. The plan explicitly acknowledges the schema rollback hazard in Remediation item 5 but dismisses it as 'an accepted tradeoff' without specifying how to revert quickly — leaving operators with no actionable recovery if the change causes unexpected INDETERMINATE verdicts at scale.
findings.location: Remediation item 5 (schema rollback note); Remediation item 3 (finalize_verdict INDETERMINATE branch); AC list generally
scenarios: A transient infrastructure issue causes widespread child-enumeration failures; all in-flight plan reviews flip to INDETERMINATE simultaneously; no flag exists to revert to the previous silent-pass behavior while the infra issue is investigated

An operator reverts the code change but the persisted precheck output records already contain hierarchy_incomplete=true; the reverted schema's additionalProperties:false rejects them; recovery paths in gate_dispatch.py throw schema-validation errors
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a boolean config/env-var (e.g., REBAR_HIERARCHY_INCOMPLETE_BLOCKS, default False during rollout, True once stable). In finalize_verdict's new elif branch: only set INDETERMINATE if the flag is enabled; otherwise emit a structured warning log and pass through (preserving today's behavior). Document: 'to rollback, set REBAR_HIERARCHY_INCOMPLETE_BLOCKS=false and redeploy; no record migration needed since hierarchy_incomplete fields are optional in the schema.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Remediation item 4 that the existing `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` guard (`verdict != BLOCK or blocking non-empty → return`) is unchanged, and the new `elif cov.get('hierarchy_incomplete')` branch fires only within that guard's scope (i.e., only when a BLOCK verdict has had its blocking bucket emptied by a drop).
evidence: Existing guard: `if verdict.get('verdict') != 'BLOCK' or verdict.get('blocking'): return` — exits unless verdict=='BLOCK' AND blocking bucket is empty

Plan text (Remediation item 4): 'add the identical `elif cov.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict["verdict"] = "INDETERMINATE"` branch so a dropped BLOCK re-derives INDETERMINATE, not PASS' — says 'a dropped BLOCK' but does not clarify the guard interaction

ABSENCE: the plan does not state that `hierarchy_incomplete` can only be consulted post-guard (i.e., only for verdicts that were BLOCK and had blocking emptied by a floor drop), leaving ambiguous whether the guard itself needs modification
findings.finding: `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` (drift_floor.py:190-211) has an early guard `if verdict.get('verdict') != 'BLOCK' or verdict.get('blocking'): return` that exits immediately unless the verdict is currently 'BLOCK' AND its blocking bucket is empty. The plan adds `elif cov.get('hierarchy_incomplete'): verdict['verdict'] = 'INDETERMINATE'` inside this function, but the plan text does not state that this branch is only reachable when a prior-BLOCK verdict has had its blocking findings dropped — creating a structural ambiguity. An executor reading the plan may (a) incorrectly widen the guard to also handle non-BLOCK verdicts, breaking the function's invariant that it never upgrades a PASS to something else, or (b) leave the guard as-is (correct) but be confused why the `hierarchy_incomplete` branch appears unreachable for verdicts that were originally INDETERMINATE (set by `finalize_verdict`'s new branch).
scenarios: An executor widens the guard (removing the `verdict != BLOCK` check) to handle the `hierarchy_incomplete` case for all verdicts — this would cause `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` to upgrade PASS→INDETERMINATE for verdicts that were never BLOCK, violating the function's documented invariant.

An executor correctly leaves the guard but is confused when testing the `hierarchy_incomplete` branch: a verdict that was originally INDETERMINATE (from the new `finalize_verdict` branch) hits the guard and returns early, so the branch appears dead — without the plan's clarification that this is correct behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Remediation item 4: 'Do NOT modify the existing early-return guard. The new `elif` fires only after a BLOCK verdict's blocking bucket is emptied by a floor drop — a verdict that was already INDETERMINATE (via `finalize_verdict`'s new branch) never reaches this function's re-derivation logic, which is correct.'

findings.finding: `gates/plan-review.yaml` is an unenumerated edit target: the plan requires `plan_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py:503) to read `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` via `ctx.inputs.get(...)`, but `ctx.inputs` is populated solely from the YAML `with:` block. The plan-review YAML has four `coach` step instances (two branch arms × two review sub-branches, at lines ~202–216 and ~298–310), each with explicit `with:` inputs sourced from `steps.precheck.outputs`. None include `hierarchy_incomplete` or `hierarchy_incomplete_detail`. Without adding those two fields to all four `coach` `with:` blocks, `ctx.inputs.get('hierarchy_incomplete', False)` will always return `False`, silently defeating the entire coach-path threading.

The plan asserts that `_reads.list_tickets` (and `list_by_query`) raises `RebarError` on any failure. In fact, `list_by_query` (the sole implementation called by `list_tickets`) invokes `ticket_reads.list_states(tracker, query)` with NO try/except wrapper, meaning `ReadError` or other exceptions from the read layer propagate as-is — not as `RebarError`. The claim 'raise a single broad `RebarError` on any failure' is therefore inaccurate for the `list_tickets` path.
findings.location: Evidence section, bullet: '`_reads.show_ticket`/`list_tickets` (`src/rebar/_reads.py:53-94`) raise a single broad `RebarError` on any failure'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Evidence bullet to: '`_reads.show_ticket` raises `RebarError` (wraps `ticket_reads.ReadError`); `_reads.list_tickets` delegates to `list_by_query` which calls `ticket_reads.list_states` with NO exception wrapper — propagating `ReadError` or other exceptions directly. Both paths are caught by the existing broad `except Exception` in `_assemble_context_uncached`; the retry predicate must remain equally broad.'

findings.finding: The plan's Evidence/Remediation text asserts that a bare `= []` default on a `@dataclass` list field raises `ValueError` at class-definition time. Python actually raises `TypeError` (not `ValueError`) for mutable defaults in dataclasses. The prescription (use `field(default_factory=list)`) is correct and consistent with `det_floor.py:114`, but the stated rationale is a false capability assertion about Python's runtime behavior that could mislead implementers checking the justification.
findings.location: Remediation item 1 — '…a bare `= []` default on a `@dataclass` list field raises `ValueError` at class-definition time…'
findings.suggested_fix: Change '…raises `ValueError` at class-definition time…' to '…raises `TypeError` at class-definition time (Python's dataclasses module: "mutable default <class list> for field X is not allowed; use default_factory")…'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
